title: Variables — giving things names
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: A variable is a name bound to a value. Assignment with = creates or rebinds the name.
explanation: When you write {user_name} = 'something', Python doesn't put the value in a box — it ties a name to it, like a label on a string. Names can be retied to new values at any time; the old value lets go quietly.
explanation_note: Written voice-neutral. The character's current personality colors delivery at runtime (pacing, asides, phrase bank interjections).
code_example:
city = "Seoul"
year = 2026
city = "Busan"   # the name 'city' now points somewhere new
print(city, year)

quiz.question: What does the code example print?

hints: Read the lines top to bottom — what happens to 'city' on line 3?
Assignment doesn't copy history. A name only remembers its latest value.
By the time print() runs, 'city' was last assigned "Busan".
